item.title: Iran war drives fertilizer costs higher as U.S. planting season nears
item.description: Illinois farmer John Yeley told CNN he can no longer get price quotes on nitrogen from most of his suppliers. NPR reported that many farmers have delayed fertilizer purchases this spring as they struggle to absorb the costs. Zippy Duvall, president of the American Farm Bureau Federation, warned that some farmers who did not preorder "may not even obtain the fertilizer that they're going to need...

item.title: Pentagon to deploy 3,000 82nd Airborne troops to Middle East
item.description: The troop movements come as President Donald Trump announced Monday that he would delay threatened strikes against Iranian power plants and energy infrastructure for five days, citing "very good and productive conversations" with Tehran over the weekend. Trump told reporters there is a "very good chance" a deal could be reached this week, with Iran's nuclear program a central demand. Iranian...

item.title: NASA cancels Lunar Gateway, will build $20B moon base
item.description:
The pivot comes as China continues advancing toward its own crewed moon landing by 2030, adding urgency to NASA's timeline. The plan also fulfills an executive order President Donald Trump signed in December directing NASA to land astronauts on the moon by 2028 and begin building a permanent outpost by 2030.
The announcement arrives roughly a week before NASA aims to launch Artemis II, which...

item.title: Pilots warned of LaGuardia hazards before deadly crash, CNN review finds
item.description: The National Transportation Safety Board has deployed a "go team" to investigate, with Canadian officials also participating. FAA Administrator Bryan Bedford described the dead pilots as "two young men at the start of their careers". New York Governor Kathy Hochul called it "an aviation disaster the likes of which we haven't seen here in three decades".

item.title: EU delays proposal to permanently ban Russian oil imports
item.description: The delay comes as the US-Israeli war on Iran creates what the International Energy Agency has called the biggest oil supply disruption in history. The closure of the Strait of Hormuz, through which roughly 20% of global oil demand typically transits, has sent crude prices surging past $100 per barrel. Fitch Ratings warned that if the strait remains closed for six months, the average Brent price...

item.title: Iran war oil crisis revives US renewable energy debate
item.description:
Even as Washington retreats from renewables, the rest of the world is accelerating. Global energy investment in 2025 reached $3.3 trillion, with two-thirds flowing into clean technologies, according to the IEA. China has seized the lead in solar manufacturing, battery production, and electric vehicles — a shift that critics say leaves the United States strategically exposed.
UN climate chief...

item.title: Iran fears ceasefire talks are a trap to kill its remaining leaders
item.description: Even if Washington were genuinely seeking dialogue, the repeated assassinations of senior officials have destroyed whatever trust remained. As one Berlin-based analyst told DW, "From Tehran's viewpoint, the harm inflicted by the U.S. is too significant to establish any trust". Iran's leadership has concluded that anyone positioned to negotiate would themselves become a target — a belief that the...

item.title: Senate nears deal to end six-week DHS shutdown after Trump meeting
item.description: Congress faces a two-week recess at the end of this week. If lawmakers leave without a deal, the shutdown could stretch past two months and become the longest closure of a federal department in history. Senate Majority Leader John Thune has warned he would keep senators in Washington if the impasse continues, though House Republican leaders have indicated they do not plan to cut their break short.

item.title: Taliban frees American academic Dennis Coyle after over a year in detention
item.description: The release comes after a string of American hostage releases from Afghanistan over the past year. In early 2025, Ryan Corbett and William McKenty were freed in exchange for a Taliban prisoner, followed by George Glezmann, Faye Hall, and Amir Amiri later that year. The Taliban have described those releases as "goodwill gestures" but had demanded the release of an Afghan al-Qaeda member held at...

item.title: Russia launches 16 Rassvet satellites to rival Starlink
item.description: The gap between Rassvet and Starlink remains vast. SpaceX crossed the 10,000 active satellite threshold just days before the Russian launch, with more than 10,000 working Starlink units now circling the planet out of over 11,500 launched since 2019. Starlink serves more than 10 million subscribers across 159 countries. Russia's 16 new satellites, while a technical achievement for Bureau 1440,...

item.title: Saudi crown prince urges Trump to escalate Iran war, deploy ground troops
item.description:
Analysts say the Crown Prince fears that if Trump pulls back now, Saudi Arabia will be left to confront an emboldened Iran alone. A half-finished offensive could expose the kingdom to continued attacks and leave Iran with the ability to periodically close the Strait of Hormuz, through which most Gulf oil reaches international markets.
"Saudi officials certainly want the war to end, but how it...

item.title: Japan to release 53M barrels from national oil reserves starting March 26
item.description:
Japan is acutely exposed: approximately 95 percent of its crude oil imports originate in the Middle East, with about 70 percent normally passing through the Hormuz chokepoint. The country maintains one of the world's largest strategic reserves, equivalent to 240 days of domestic consumption.
Even as reserves flow, uncertainty persists. Chevron CEO Mike Wirth warned at CERAWeek that "it will...

item.title: Gallup: Only 28% of US workers say it's a good time to find a quality job
item.description: The Gallup survey also found that workers' assessments of their current lives and future prospects have fallen to their dimmest level since the firm began measuring workforce well-being in 2009. For the first time in Gallup's tracking, more workers report struggling in their lives (49%) than thriving (46%). The Conference Board's consumer confidence index stood at just 91.2 in February, close to...

item.title: Israel to occupy southern Lebanon up to Litani River, defence minister says
item.description: Hezbollah drew Lebanon into the broader regional conflict when it began firing rockets into Israel on March 2, following Israel's targeted killing of Iranian Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei in the opening strikes of a U.S.-Israeli military operation against Iran on February 28. Israeli strikes across Lebanon have since killed more than 1,000 people and displaced over a million, according to Reuters....

item.title: China mapping ocean floor for submarine warfare, Reuters finds
item.description: The mapping campaign is one element of a broader Chinese push to challenge long-standing U.S. undersea dominance. China is also building what analysts have called an "Underwater Great Wall" — a layered network of fixed and mobile sensors in strategically vital chokepoints — while modernizing its submarine fleet with next-generation Type 095 attack submarines and Type 096 ballistic missile...

item.title: Sunken Soviet sub is leaking radioactive material, study finds
item.description:
A 2024 investigation by the U.S. Department of Energy's Pacific Northwest National Laboratory confirmed that storm surges and gradual sea-level rise pose the greatest threat to spreading radionuclides across Enewetak Atoll. Most of Runit Island sits barely two meters above sea level. Climate scientists project water levels around the Marshall Islands could rise by a meter by 2100.
The...

item.title: Iran fires missiles at Israel as IRGC rejects Trump's peace talk claims
item.description: The conflict, which began on February 28 with what Trump called "major combat operations" against Iran, has engulfed much of the region. Iran has attacked all six Gulf Cooperation Council countries for the first time in history, striking airports, oil facilities, and U.S. military bases across the UAE, Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, Bahrain, Qatar, and Oman. The Strait of Hormuz, through which roughly...

item.title: Magnitude 7.6 earthquake strikes near Tonga; no tsunami threat
item.description: While the deep focus of Tuesday's earthquake reduced the risk of surface damage, seismologists continue to monitor the region. Tonga, a Polynesian monarchy comprising more than 170 islands in the South Pacific, remains vulnerable to seismic and volcanic hazards given its position along a major subduction zone where the Pacific plate dives beneath the Australian plate.

item.title: AWS Bahrain region disrupted by drones for second time this month
item.description: The repeated strikes have raised questions about the vulnerability of hyperscale cloud infrastructure to military conflict. The Uptime Institute described the March 1 incident as the first confirmed military attack on a hyperscale cloud provider. The Center for Strategic and International Studies warned that data centers and supporting energy infrastructure could become targets in the "compute...

item.title: Saudi Arabia, UAE edge toward joining war on Iran
item.description:
The escalation comes as Iran has expanded its targeting to Gulf energy assets. After Israel struck Iran's South Pars gas field on March 18, Tehran declared facilities in Saudi Arabia, the UAE, and Qatar "direct and legitimate targets," according to Al Jazeera. Qatar's Ras Laffan LNG complex and Abu Dhabi's Habshan Gas Facility were subsequently hit.
Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman is now...
